title: Installing coving and cornice
subtitle: Measure, cut the mitres, stick it in place
excerpt: How to fit plaster or polystyrene coving to the wall-ceiling junction: marking the working lines, cutting the 45-degree corner mitres, mixing adhesive, and buttering the profile so it bonds cleanly.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Knauf Danoline technical guidance (public domain). City and Guilds Level 2 Plastering and Decorating syllabus.

glossaryTerms.definition: A moulded strip of plaster or polystyrene fitted at the junction between wall and ceiling. Decorative in period rooms; in modern houses it also covers small cracks at that junction.

content.text: is an afternoon job when the walls are square and the room has simple corners. In period houses with wavy plaster or out-of-square corners, allow a full day. The work splits into three stages: preparing the wall-ceiling junction, cutting the mitres, and bonding the lengths in place. The mitre cuts are the part most beginners get wrong; the section below covers the internal and external corner cuts for the two standard coving orientations.

Polystyrene is lighter and less brittle to cut; plaster gives a harder, denser result. Both are available in 2 m and 3 m lengths from builders' merchants. Choose a profile to match existing plasterwork.
A ready-mixed plaster-based adhesive. Sold in 4 kg tubs. One tub covers a medium room.
Tenon saw and coving mitre box.
Most large builders' merchants loan or include a plastic mitre box sized for the specific coving profile.
For marking the working lines on wall and ceiling.
For cleaning the bonding surfaces and filling gaps after fitting.
For spreading adhesive and tooling joints.

Hold a piece of coving in the corner and mark a pencil line along the bottom edge on the wall and the top edge on the ceiling. These lines define where the adhesive will go. Work around the room, marking all four sides before cutting.

Scrape back any loose paint or flaking plaster inside the two guide lines. Roughen a shiny or glossy surface with 80-grit sandpaper. Vacuum and wipe the area clean. The coving will not stick to dust or grease.

Measure each wall from corner to corner. Cut the coving lengths square at both ends for walls without external corners. The saw cut is 90 degrees across the profile; the coving mitre box holds the piece at the correct angle for the cut.
Step 4, cut the internal corner mitres.

An internal corner (a standard room corner) needs a pair of 45-degree cuts that overlap when placed in the corner. The coving mitre box has marked positions for left-hand and right-hand internal corners. Cut one piece for each side of the corner, fitting the longer piece first. Offer both pieces into the corner before applying adhesive to check the join closes without a gap. If it does not close, the corner is not square; adjust by paring the back of the mitre with the filling knife.

Butter the back rebate of the coving with adhesive using the 100 mm filling knife, pressing it into both the wall and ceiling edges of the back channel. Apply enough adhesive that a small amount is squeezed out when the coving is pressed home. Do not apply so much that the adhesive sags onto the face.

Press the coving firmly into position between the two pencil lines. Run a screwdriver or filler knife along the outside face, pressing upward and inward, to be sure the adhesive makes contact along the full length. Hold for 30 to 60 seconds. With plaster coving, a strip of masking tape from wall to ceiling can support the piece while the adhesive grabs.

Once the adhesive has set (minimum 2 hours), fill the butt joints and any corner gaps with coving adhesive or lightweight filler. Run a wet finger along the filled joint to smooth it. Wipe away any smeared adhesive from the face with a damp cloth before it hardens.

Allow the adhesive to cure fully before painting, at minimum overnight, longer in cold or damp conditions. Apply a diluted mist coat of emulsion first; bare plaster and polystyrene coving are both absorbent and will soak a full-strength coat unevenly. One mist coat and one full coat is the standard sequence.

External corners (a chimney breast edge, a door-head projection) need the coving to turn outward rather than inward. The external corner mitre cut is the mirror of the internal cut; cut with the piece in the external-corner position in the mitre box.

To replace a single damaged length, score along the top and bottom edges of the coving with a craft knife, then prise it away. Clean all the old adhesive back to sound plaster before fitting the new length.
In older houses with run-in-place plaster cornices, polystyrene will not match. Fibrous plaster to match a period profile can be cast from a section of the original; this is a specialist job.

attrs.symptom: Coving drops off the wall within 24 hours
attrs.cause: Bonding surface was too smooth, too dusty, or had loose paint.
attrs.fix: Remove the piece. Scrape and sand the wall and ceiling surfaces back to sound plaster. Re-apply with fresh adhesive.

attrs.symptom: Mitre joint gaps will not close
attrs.cause: Corner is out of square or the cut angle was slightly off.
attrs.fix: Pare the back of the mitre cut with a sharp filling knife to adjust the angle. A small gap at the back is fine; a gap at the face reads as a fault.

attrs.symptom: Adhesive shows on the face of the coving
attrs.cause: Too much adhesive applied, or the piece was pressed in at an angle.
attrs.fix: Wipe immediately with a damp cloth before the adhesive sets. If it has set, careful scraping with the filling knife followed by light sanding removes it without scoring polystyrene deeply.

attrs.symptom: Paint refuses to cover uniformly on plaster coving
attrs.cause: No mist coat applied; the bare plaster is drawing paint in at different rates.
attrs.fix: Sand lightly, apply a diluted mist coat, and allow to dry completely before applying the full-strength coat.
